paper_title: Study of Pinguicula vulgaris in a Subarctic Geothermal Area

methods.method_name: Marking and Monitoring of P. vulgaris Individuals
methods.description: This method involves marking individuals of P. vulgaris and monitoring their floral development to study flowering phenology and plant fitness.

steps.description: 450 individuals of P. vulgaris were marked with a numbered plastic tag and their position recorded on a map.
steps.searchable_summary: mark and map P. vulgaris individuals

steps.description: Soil temperature at a depth of 10 cm was measured near each plant using a digital thermometer.
Floral development was monitored weekly, and each plant was assigned to one of nine floral development stages.
steps.searchable_summary: assign floral development stages to plants

steps.description: Estimate the first flowering date (FFD) using the mean of the last recording with buds and the first recording with flowers.
Adjust FFD estimates using development stages at last recording with buds and first recording with flowers.
steps.searchable_summary: adjust FFD estimates based on development stages

steps.description: Count the number of flowering stems and measure the longest diameter of the rosette and the perpendicular diameter.
steps.searchable_summary: measure plant dimensions and count stems

steps.description: Count the number of mature intact fruits and collect all fruits for seed counting.
steps.searchable_summary: count and collect fruits for seed counting

steps.description: Count seeds in each fruit to measure plant fitness.
steps.searchable_summary: count seeds to assess plant fitness

methods.method_name: Experimental Manipulation of Pollination and Prey Availability
methods.description: This method involves manipulating pollination and prey availability to assess their effects on plant fitness and phenotypic selection.

steps.description: Randomly assign flowering individuals to one of four treatments: pollen supplementation, prey supplementation, both, or control.
Hand-pollinate all open flowers with a brush using a mix of pollen from multiple individuals.
Add three Simulium vittatum flies per week to plants for prey supplementation.
Use Drosophila melanogaster flies as a supplement when Simulium availability is low.
steps.searchable_summary: supplement with Drosophila flies when needed

steps.description: Place flies under the curved leaf margins to maximize contact with the digestive surface.
